case study · hotel pms · digistay (current role)

cutting front-desk training ~40% on a hotel pms that runs 30,000+ check-ins a month

eight months at digistay, eight modules shipped. this is the story of the hardest one — guest accounts — told with real production screens, plus the rbac and night audit systems built around it.

live in production · ~30,000 check-ins a month
role senior product designer, research to handoff, alongside engineering surfaces web · mobile
~30,000
check-ins a month run through these screens
500
active hotel staff across roles
8 in 8
modules shipped in eight months

user interviews every month, as a habit
why it matters

a hotel runs on one number at the end of the day: does the bill match reality?

every feature here was pulled from somewhere real — sales requirements, support escalations, and 15–20 user interviews a month against a base of 500 active staff users. the outcomes are measured, not estimated: usage from mixpanel, operations from the company's mis. when i say a number on this page, that's where it comes from.

the problem

ever split a bill at an office party? food goes on the company card, drinks on your own. one table, one order — two bills.

hotels face that exact situation every day, at scale. a corporate guest checks in on a company booking: the company pays for the room, the guest pays for their own food and extras. but hotel software drops every charge onto one folio. so at checkout, the front desk untangles it by hand — line by line, guest standing at the counter, client invoice one mistake away from going out wrong. in our monthly interviews, hoteliers brought this up again and again.

the hotelier

owns the invoice · answers for errors

needs company charges and personal charges separated from the first minute of the stay, not untangled at the last.

the small property

no corporate traffic · wants zero overhead

doesn't care about any of this. any solution that adds a mandatory step for them is a solution that fails.

the fix · guest accounts

the fix: separate running bills inside a single booking.

one guest account per room, created automatically. hotels that never split bills see nothing new. hotels that do can open as many accounts as a client needs — food on one, minibar on another — and every charge is placed on its account the moment it happens. by checkout, the two bills already exist. the front desk just prints them.

see it running

the same flow, recorded live in production.

one minute, unedited: the guest account opens, charges get selected, moved to their bill, and the confirmation lands — exactly the steps you just read.

the journey · how the modules hook together

guest accounts don't work alone. follow the booking through the system and every module takes its turn.

front desk system guest night audit
front desk
the corporate booking arrives
three rooms, room-and-tax to the company, extras to the guests. reservation created.
system · rbac
roles decide who touches what
desk, housekeeping, auditor, manager — every action gated by an auditable permission model across 10+ roles.
system · guest account
one account per room, automatically
the split-billing machinery exists from minute one — invisible until someone needs it.
front desk · check-in
guests arrive, accounts take shape
the desk adds a food account for the client; every room-service order, minibar item and laundry charge is placed to its account as it happens.
guest
the stay just happens
early check-in, add-ons, discounts — each one lands on the right wallet without anyone doing accounting in their head.
night audit
the day closes, and the numbers must agree
the night-shift user checks and balances the property at 2am — the module that has to be trusted half-asleep.
system · tax & rates
the money logic settles
rate plans price the stay; the tax module computes gst per line so the invoice is right by construction, not by correction.
front desk · checkout
two invoices, zero reconstruction
company bill to the company, personal bill to the guest — the split was made all along, so checkout is just printing the truth.

every stop on this timeline has its chapter below — rbac, night audit, and the hours where the money logic lives.

chapter two · rbac

before anyone splits a bill, the system has to know who's allowed to touch it.

a hotel isn't one user — it's a front desk, housekeeping, a restaurant, an auditor and a manager, all in the same system with very different rights. the permission model runs three layers deep: modules a role can enter, screens it can view, actions it can take. and because "who did what" matters as much as "who can do what," every change lands in an audit log.

chapter three · night audit

at 2am, someone has to close the day — and the numbers have to agree before tomorrow is allowed to start.

night audit is the hotel's daily financial close: every departure settled, every no-show decided, every invoice written, before the business date rolls over. the old way is a checklist in someone's head. i designed it as a guided close — four queues with live counts, and the day doesn't end until every one of them reads zero.

chapter four · the hours between stays

a guest arrives at 7am. the room was free all night. those hours are either revenue or a gift — and most hotels gave them away by accident.

early check-in and late check-out sound like a scheduling feature. they're a pricing feature. the design job was to let each property set its own policy once — which hours cost what — and then have every reservation, time change and invoice obey it automatically, with the tax module keeping the gst right on top.

decisions underneath

  1. opt-in complexity, invisible by default

    one account per room is created automatically, so hotels that never split a bill never meet the feature. the machinery exists; the burden doesn't.

  2. n accounts, not a company/personal toggle

    the obvious design is two buckets. real clients ask for three, four, five. designing for n cost little and killed a whole class of future requests.

  3. split at the source, not at checkout

    every charge picks its account the moment it's added. the alternative — reconstructing intent at checkout — is exactly the error-prone mess we were replacing.

  4. combined or specific, one control at invoice time

    the invoice screen asks a single question: all accounts, or this one? everything else — gst per line, round-off — is computed, not typed.

  5. moves are visible, not silent

    when charges move between accounts, the result is shown in place. in money flows, a vanishing toast is not confirmation.

how it was grounded

fifteen to twenty user interviews a month, every month.

with 500 active staff users, research isn't a phase here — it's a standing appointment. guest accounts came out of exactly this loop: sales kept relaying the same corporate-billing complaint, interviews with front-desk staff showed the checkout reconstruction happening by hand, and mixpanel showed where time was going. the feature shipped, and the same loop now measures it.

what shipped · eight modules in eight months

guest accounts is one chapter. the full system i've designed at digistay — all eight modules live, in daily use — from the same design system of 200+ components:

guest account● shipped · this case study
rbac● shipped
night audit● shipped
tax module● shipped
log in / sign up● shipped
advance room management● shipped
early check-in & late check-out● shipped
rate plan● shipped

front-desk training time is down ~40% since the redesign, measured against the mis baseline. the rest of the numbers live in mixpanel dashboards i'm happy to walk through in a conversation.

what surprised me, what i'd still change

the thing that surprised me: nobody asked for "guest accounts." they asked for clean invoices. the feature name, the model, the n-accounts shape — all of that came from listening past the request to the workflow underneath it.

what i'd change next: charge placement is still a decision the desk makes each time. the obvious evolution is defaults per charge type — food to the food account unless overridden — so the common case becomes zero decisions. it's on the roadmap.

the best compliment this feature gets is that most hotels never notice it exists — and the ones who need it can't imagine the desk without it.
